industries.name: Artisanal & Small-Scale Gold Mining (ASGM)
industries.share: ~38% of global mercury consumption — largest single use
uses: Mercury amalgamation: miners mix liquid mercury with gold-bearing ore to form gold-mercury amalgam
Amalgam is then heated (often with a torch) to vaporize the mercury and recover the gold
An estimated 10–19 million miners in 70+ countries use this method — primarily informal sector
Releases ~1,000 tonnes of Hg into environment annually; largest global source of mercury pollution
Minamata Convention requires nations to reduce and ultimately eliminate ASGM mercury use

companies.name: UN Environment Programme (UNEP)
companies.volume: Tracks ~1,500 t Hg/yr in this sector
companies.use: Coordinates global monitoring of ASGM mercury use through the Global Mercury Partnership. Funds national action plans and supports transitions to mercury-free mining techniques.

companies.name: Artisanal Gold Council
companies.use: NGO developing mercury-free processing technology (direct smelting, shaking tables) and deploying it in active ASGM regions in South America, Africa, and Southeast Asia.

companies.name: UNIDO — United Nations Industrial Development Organization
companies.use: Implements the Global Mercury Project and National Action Plans for ASGM mercury reduction. Has trained thousands of miners in mercury-free methods across 7 target countries.

companies.name: Pure Earth (Blacksmith Institute)
companies.use: Environmental nonprofit remediating mercury contamination from ASGM sites in low- and middle-income countries. Also funds community health programs for mercury-exposed populations.

industries.name: Vinyl Chloride Monomer (VCM (vinyl chloride monomer)) Production
industries.share: ~26% of global mercury consumption — China-specific industrial use
uses: Mercury(II) chloride catalyst converts acetylene (from coal via calcium carbide) to vinyl chloride monomer (VCM (vinyl chloride monomer))
VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) is the precursor to PVC — one of the world's most-produced plastics
China's coal-rich regions use this acetylene-based route rather than the ethylene route used elsewhere
Approximately 700,000–800,000 tonnes of mercury-based catalyst in use across Chinese PVC plants
China has committed to transition to mercury-free gold-based catalysts under Minamata obligations

companies.name: Xinjiang Zhongtai Chemical
companies.share: ~12% of acetylene-based VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) capacity
companies.use: One of China's largest PVC producers using the acetylene-to-VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) route. Xinjiang's coal resources make this route economical despite mercury intensity. Largest single mercury consumer for VCM (vinyl chloride monomer).

companies.name: Xinjiang Tianye Group
companies.share: ~7% of acetylene-based VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) capacity
companies.use: Major Xinjiang-based chemical company with calcium carbide / acetylene / VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) integrated operations. Has piloted low-mercury catalyst trials to reduce Hg loading per tonne of PVC.

companies.name: Ordos Junzheng Energy & Chemical
companies.share: ~6% of acetylene-based VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) capacity
companies.use: Inner Mongolia's largest coal-chemical group. Produces PVC via the acetylene route using mercury catalysts. Subject to Minamata-driven production limits under China's chemical sector regulations.

companies.name: Johnson Matthey
companies.use: Has developed gold-chloride catalyst technology to replace mercury in VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) production. Supplying AuCl₃ (gold chloride)-based catalyst to Chinese PVC plants as mercury phase-down alternative.

companies.name: BASF SE
companies.use: Research and development partner for mercury-free VCM (vinyl chloride monomer) catalysts. Not a mercury consumer in this sector, but supplies catalyst transition technology and chemical engineering services.

industries.name: Fluorescent & Specialty Lighting
uses: Low-pressure mercury vapor in fluorescent tubes and compact fluorescent lamps (CFLs) — 3–10 mg Hg each
High-intensity discharge (HID) lamps: metal halide, mercury vapor — 20–200 mg Hg each
Ultra-high-pressure mercury (UHPM (ultra-high-pressure mercury)) lamps in DLP projectors, UV curing, and lithography tools
Mercury-xenon arc lamps for solar simulators and optical inspection systems
Germicidal UV-C lamps using mercury vapor for water purification and air disinfection

companies.name: Osram
companies.use: Global lighting manufacturer still producing mercury-based specialty lamps for professional and industrial applications (projectors, UV curing, medical). LED transition has cut mercury use sharply.

companies.name: Signify (Philips Lighting)
companies.use: Former world leader in fluorescent lighting; rapidly transitioning to LED. Still manufactures mercury-based UV-C germicidal lamps for water treatment and food safety applications.

companies.name: Trojan Technologies
companies.use: Leading manufacturer of mercury UV lamps for drinking water and wastewater disinfection. Is transitioning municipal customers to UV-LED technology, reducing mercury dependency.

companies.name: Heraeus
companies.use: Specialty mercury lamp producer for semiconductor lithography (KrF excimer lamp precursors), UV curing systems, and scientific instruments. Supplies UHPM (ultra-high-pressure mercury) lamps for EUV research.

companies.name: Nichia Corporation
companies.use: World's largest LED maker, but retains some fluorescent lamp production for legacy markets. Investing heavily in UV-C LED to replace mercury germicidal lamps in medical and industrial settings.

industries.name: Dental Amalgam
industries.share: ~3% of global mercury consumption (declining under Minamata)
uses: Dental amalgam: ~50% mercury + silver, tin, copper alloy — used for posterior tooth restorations
Amalgam is self-setting, highly durable (15–30 year lifespan), and low-cost compared to alternatives
Minamata Convention Phase-Down: 140+ countries committed to reducing amalgam use by 2030
Alternatives (composite resin, glass ionomer, ceramics) are increasingly preferred for anterior and pediatric use
Amalgam waste from dental clinics is a significant mercury release pathway into wastewater

companies.name: Dentsply Sirona
companies.use: World's largest dental products company and the leading producer of dental amalgam (Dispersalloy, Tytin brands). Has committed to provide amalgam alternatives and phase down production.

companies.name: SDI Limited
companies.use: One of the few remaining large-scale amalgam manufacturers globally. Also markets composite alternatives (Aura, Palfique) as part of its transition strategy under Minamata obligations.

companies.name: GC Corporation
companies.use: Japanese dental materials maker with amalgam products primarily for Asian and developing markets. Also manufactures glass ionomer (GC Fuji) as a mercury-free alternative.

companies.name: Kerr Dental (Envista Holdings)
companies.use: Produces Tytin amalgam alloy for dental restorations. Envista has committed to providing non-amalgam alternatives and supporting the transition away from mercury-containing dental materials.

companies.name: Solstice Dental (Coltene)
companies.use: Niche amalgam producer for specialty dental markets. Coltene has prioritized composite and glass ionomer product lines as part of its move away from mercury-containing restoratives.

industries.name: Chlor-Alkali Production
industries.share: ~4% of global mercury consumption (largely phased out in West)
uses: Mercury amalgam electrolysis cells produce chlorine gas and sodium hydroxide (caustic soda)
Amalgam cells were once dominant; now largely replaced by membrane and diaphragm cells
EU closed all mercury chlor-alkali plants by 2017; USA and Japan phased out by 2015
Some remaining mercury chlor-alkali capacity persists in India, China, and parts of Latin America
Each mercury cell plant contains 1,000–3,000 tonnes of liquid mercury — requiring careful decommissioning

companies.name: Covestro
companies.use: Former operator of mercury chlor-alkali plants in Brunsbuettel and Leverkusen, Germany (closed 2014–2016). Still managing legacy mercury waste cleanup at former facility sites.

companies.name: Olin Corporation
companies.use: Largest US chlor-alkali producer; completed transition away from mercury amalgam cells to membrane technology. Manages historical mercury-contaminated sites from legacy manufacturing.

companies.name: Tronox Holdings
companies.use: Chemical company managing legacy mercury obligations from former Kerr-McGee and Huntsman chlor-alkali facilities. Mercury in legacy use context — not active production.

companies.name: Gujarat Alkalies and Chemicals (GACL)
companies.use: India's largest chlor-alkali producer. Has been transitioning from mercury cells to membrane cells at its Vadodara and Dahej facilities as part of India's Minamata compliance program.
